title: How Europe Can Get Putin’s Attention
gist: Gabuev argues Europe can no longer outsource crisis diplomacy with Russia to a Trump-led Washington and should form a small France-Germany-UK-plus-Finland coalition to open a direct high-level channel to Putin—stabilizing relations and averting an uncontrolled arms race even as it keeps arming Ukraine.

insights.claim: Europe must open its own high-level channel to Putin because it can no longer rely on Washington to manage crisis diplomacy with Russia, a task the U.S. handled competently under officials like former CIA director Bill Burns before Trump filled the role with neophytes.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: A structural shift in who manages escalation risk in Europe—watch for European-led back-channels replacing U.S. crisis diplomacy.

insights.claim: Even long-hawkish pro-Ukraine leaders now favor talking to the Kremlin, with Finland's Stubb saying 'I believe we should talk to Putin,' Macron advocating it in February, and Meloni in June, motivated by escalation risk as much as ending the war.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: A cross-European shift toward re-engagement with Moscow signals de-escalation diplomacy is becoming politically mainstream.
insights.quote: I believe we should talk to Putin
insights.locator: “I believe we should talk to Putin,”

insights.claim: Under Trump, U.S. crisis diplomacy with Russia passed to figures like Steve Witkoff, a real estate developer with no government experience, while the White House threatened to pull back from NATO and even annex Greenland.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: The competence gap in U.S. Russia diplomacy raises miscalculation risk that Europe now feels compelled to backstop.

insights.claim: With Finland and Sweden in NATO, warning times for potential missile attack have collapsed and remaining Russia-NATO arms control deals have been destroyed, putting the two sides at risk of replaying the 1970s-80s European missile crisis with no guardrails.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: Shortened warning times plus growing arsenals mean elevated tail risk of accidental confrontation on the continent.

insights.claim: Both Russia and Europe are scaling up missile and drone arsenals and relying more on automation and AI, which Gabuev warns is prone to glitches and outside manipulation, raising the odds of miscalculation and escalation.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: AI-enabled automated defense raises systemic escalation risk that pure rearmament cannot resolve.

insights.claim: Gabuev recommends a coalition of France, Germany, and the United Kingdom—plus an eastern-flank state, most likely Finland since Poland's president and prime minister are locked in conflict—to lead direct engagement with Putin.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: Names the specific European power configuration most likely to drive any future Russia negotiation.

insights.claim: The coalition should reach Putin via a personal, confidential letter to avoid the emotional missteps of in-person or video engagement, proposing hotlines and teams to manage flash points like airspace violations and undersea cable cuts.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: A concrete low-risk diplomatic tactic that could re-establish Russia-Europe communication channels.

insights.claim: Gabuev judges the chances of Trump making a deal favoring Putin have grown 'quite slim and only growing slimmer,' noting Trump signaled at a June G-7 summit willingness to reimpose oil sanctions on Russia.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: Cuts against the consensus fear that Trump will hand Putin a favorable settlement.

insights.claim: Ukraine is now mostly holding the frontline and inflicting more pain on Russia, as its June strikes on Moscow showed, while Europe has stepped in to provide the largest share of assistance to Kyiv.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: A battlefield stabilization plus European burden-shift weakens Moscow's negotiating leverage.

insights.claim: Even Putin loyalists such as Herman Gref, CEO of Russia's largest bank, have dared to challenge the Kremlin's decision to keep fighting rather than seeking an off-ramp, signaling growing elite unease.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: Elite dissent is a leading indicator that Moscow's war consensus is fraying—watch Russian business elites.
insights.locator: by acting now, Europe might be able to affect

insights.claim: Gabuev expects any serious deal on European security architecture will most likely have to wait until 2029 given Trump's lack of focus and expertise, though direct European-Putin engagement can lay the groundwork and manage risk now.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year horizon for any grand bargain, with interim risk-management as the realistic near-term goal.
insights.locator: With Trump in charge, Washington lacks the focus

insights.claim: Gabuev urges Europe to establish Kremlin channels now while its current leaders cooperate well, before populist forces such as Germany's AfD win more elections and make the next generation of European leadership less collaborative.
insights.speaker: Alexander Gabuev
insights.so_what: The window for coordinated European diplomacy may close as populists gain ground—a timing signal.
